summary: Gruber argues against the idea of Apple creating a luxury ceramic 'iPhone Edition' similar to the Apple Watch Edition, contending that the iPhone's brand strength lies in its egalitarian nature — everyone gets the same best phone. He then dismantles the widespread speculation that Apple would create something special for the iPhone's 10th anniversary in 2017, arguing that Apple doesn't hold back technology for marketing gimmicks and always ships the best product it can as soon as it's ready. He compares the iPhone to Warhol's observation about Coca-Cola: its democratizing quality is a feature, not a limitation. Any new design appearing in 2017 would be coincidental timing, not anniversary planning.
key_points: A luxury iPhone Edition would undermine the brand's egalitarian appeal — unlike watches, phones have never had a luxury tier tradition
Apple doesn't hold back technology for anniversaries; they ship the best product they can as fast as they can
The 20th Anniversary Macintosh is a cautionary tale from old Apple heading toward bankruptcy, not a precedent to follow
If a new edge-to-edge iPhone design appears in 2017, the timing with the 10th anniversary would be purely coincidental
New Apple products are marketed as new — anniversaries are about getting old
money_quotes: An expensive limited edition iPhone would mean most iPhone buyers would know they're only getting second best.
Apple is not going to make a special edition of any product — let alone the iPhone, their most important product — just to mark an anniversary.
To maintain its position as the leading phone-maker in the world, Apple must push forward as fast as they can. They only know one way to play the game: as hard as they can.
Nothing gets held back from any Apple product just to make the next one more special.
Every year, Apple releases the best iPhone it is able to make. That's it.
New iPhones — new Apple products, period — are marketed as new. Anniversaries are about getting old.
I'll enjoy a nice serving of homemade claim chowder if Apple goes and names next year's iPhone the 'iPhone 10' and makes the anniversary central to its branding.
key_insight: The iPhone's brand power comes from being the best phone everyone can buy equally, and Apple's competitive position demands shipping the best product possible each year regardless of arbitrary calendar milestones.
